title: Divide complex numbers
description: Given a complex number division, express the result as a complex number of the form a+bi.
topic_path: Complex conjugates and dividing complex numbers

hints: We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{5-3i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-32+8i}{5+3i} = \dfrac{-32+8i}{5+3i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{5-3i}}{\green{5-3i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-32+8i) \cdot (5-3i)} {5^2 - (3i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-32+8i) \cdot (5-3i)} {(5)^2 - (3i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-32+8i) \cdot (5-3i)} {25 + 9} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-32+8i) \cdot (5-3i)} {34} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-32+8i}) \cdot (\red{5-3i})} {34} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-32} \cdot \red{5} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{5 i} + \blue{-32} \cdot \red{-3 i} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{-3 i^2}} {34} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-160 + 40i + 96i - 24 i^2} {34} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-160 + 40i + 96i + 24} {34} = \dfrac{-136 + 136i} {34} = -4+4i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{5-i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-23+11i}{5+i} = \dfrac{-23+11i}{5+i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{5-i}}{\green{5-i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-23+11i) \cdot (5-i)} {5^2 - (i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-23+11i) \cdot (5-i)} {(5)^2 - (i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-23+11i) \cdot (5-i)} {25 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-23+11i) \cdot (5-i)} {26} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-23+11i}) \cdot (\red{5-i})} {26} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-23} \cdot \red{5} + \blue{11} \cdot \red{5 i} + \blue{-23} \cdot \red{-1 i} + \blue{11} \cdot \red{-1 i^2}} {26} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-115 + 55i + 23i - 11 i^2} {26} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-115 + 55i + 23i + 11} {26} = \dfrac{-104 + 78i} {26} = -4+3i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{3-2i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-20+4i}{3+2i} = \dfrac{-20+4i}{3+2i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{3-2i}}{\green{3-2i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-20+4i) \cdot (3-2i)} {3^2 - (2i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-20+4i) \cdot (3-2i)} {(3)^2 - (2i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-20+4i) \cdot (3-2i)} {9 + 4} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-20+4i) \cdot (3-2i)} {13} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-20+4i}) \cdot (\red{3-2i})} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-20} \cdot \red{3} + \blue{4} \cdot \red{3 i} + \blue{-20} \cdot \red{-2 i} + \blue{4} \cdot \red{-2 i^2}} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-60 + 12i + 40i - 8 i^2} {13} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-60 + 12i + 40i + 8} {13} = \dfrac{-52 + 52i} {13} = -4+4i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-2+4i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-14+22i}{-2-4i} = \dfrac{-14+22i}{-2-4i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-2+4i}}{\green{-2+4i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-14+22i) \cdot (-2+4i)} {(-2)^2 - (-4i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-14+22i) \cdot (-2+4i)} {(-2)^2 - (-4i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-14+22i) \cdot (-2+4i)} {4 + 16} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-14+22i) \cdot (-2+4i)} {20} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-14+22i}) \cdot (\red{-2+4i})} {20} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-14} \cdot \red{(-2)} + \blue{22} \cdot \red{(-2) i} + \blue{-14} \cdot \red{4 i} + \blue{22} \cdot \red{4 i^2}} {20} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{28 - 44i - 56i + 88 i^2} {20} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{28 - 44i - 56i - 88} {20} = \dfrac{-60 - 100i} {20} = -3-5i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{1+2i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-11-3i}{1-2i} = \dfrac{-11-3i}{1-2i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{1+2i}}{\green{1+2i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-11-3i) \cdot (1+2i)} {1^2 - (-2i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-11-3i) \cdot (1+2i)} {(1)^2 - (-2i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-11-3i) \cdot (1+2i)} {1 + 4} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-11-3i) \cdot (1+2i)} {5} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-11-3i}) \cdot (\red{1+2i})} {5} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-11} \cdot \red{1} + \blue{-3} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{-11} \cdot \red{2 i} + \blue{-3} \cdot \red{2 i^2}} {5} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-11 - 3i - 22i - 6 i^2} {5} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-11 - 3i - 22i + 6} {5} = \dfrac{-5 - 25i} {5} = -1-5i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-3+2i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-9-6i}{-3-2i} = \dfrac{-9-6i}{-3-2i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-3+2i}}{\green{-3+2i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-9-6i) \cdot (-3+2i)} {(-3)^2 - (-2i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-9-6i) \cdot (-3+2i)} {(-3)^2 - (-2i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-9-6i) \cdot (-3+2i)} {9 + 4} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-9-6i) \cdot (-3+2i)} {13} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-9-6i}) \cdot (\red{-3+2i})} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-9} \cdot \red{(-3)} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{(-3) i} + \blue{-9} \cdot \red{2 i} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{2 i^2}} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{27 + 18i - 18i - 12 i^2} {13} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{27 + 18i - 18i + 12} {13} = \dfrac{39 + 0i} {13} = 3 $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{4+4i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-8+24i}{4-4i} = \dfrac{-8+24i}{4-4i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{4+4i}}{\green{4+4i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8+24i) \cdot (4+4i)} {4^2 - (-4i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8+24i) \cdot (4+4i)} {(4)^2 - (-4i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8+24i) \cdot (4+4i)} {16 + 16} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8+24i) \cdot (4+4i)} {32} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-8+24i}) \cdot (\red{4+4i})} {32} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-8} \cdot \red{4} + \blue{24} \cdot \red{4 i} + \blue{-8} \cdot \red{4 i} + \blue{24} \cdot \red{4 i^2}} {32} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-32 + 96i - 32i + 96 i^2} {32} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-32 + 96i - 32i - 96} {32} = \dfrac{-128 + 64i} {32} = -4+2i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{3-i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-8-6i}{3+i} = \dfrac{-8-6i}{3+i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{3-i}}{\green{3-i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8-6i) \cdot (3-i)} {3^2 - (i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8-6i) \cdot (3-i)} {(3)^2 - (i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8-6i) \cdot (3-i)} {9 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-8-6i) \cdot (3-i)} {10} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-8-6i}) \cdot (\red{3-i})} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-8} \cdot \red{3} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{3 i} + \blue{-8} \cdot \red{-1 i} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{-1 i^2}} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-24 - 18i + 8i + 6 i^2} {10} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-24 - 18i + 8i - 6} {10} = \dfrac{-30 - 10i} {10} = -3-i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-4+3i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-6+8i}{-4-3i} = \dfrac{-6+8i}{-4-3i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-4+3i}}{\green{-4+3i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-6+8i) \cdot (-4+3i)} {(-4)^2 - (-3i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-6+8i) \cdot (-4+3i)} {(-4)^2 - (-3i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-6+8i) \cdot (-4+3i)} {16 + 9} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-6+8i) \cdot (-4+3i)} {25} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-6+8i}) \cdot (\red{-4+3i})} {25} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-6} \cdot \red{(-4)} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{(-4) i} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{3 i} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{3 i^2}} {25} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{24 - 32i - 18i + 24 i^2} {25} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{24 - 32i - 18i - 24} {25} = \dfrac{0 - 50i} {25} = -2i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{1+i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-4-2i}{1-i} = \dfrac{-4-2i}{1-i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{1+i}}{\green{1+i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-4-2i) \cdot (1+i)} {1^2 - (-i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-4-2i) \cdot (1+i)} {(1)^2 - (-i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-4-2i) \cdot (1+i)} {1 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-4-2i) \cdot (1+i)} {2} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-4-2i}) \cdot (\red{1+i})} {2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-4} \cdot \red{1} + \blue{-2} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{-4} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{-2} \cdot \red{1 i^2}} {2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-4 - 2i - 4i - 2 i^2} {2} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-4 - 2i - 4i + 2} {2} = \dfrac{-2 - 6i} {2} = -1-3i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-2-3i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-3-15i}{-2+3i} = \dfrac{-3-15i}{-2+3i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-2-3i}}{\green{-2-3i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3-15i) \cdot (-2-3i)} {(-2)^2 - (3i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3-15i) \cdot (-2-3i)} {(-2)^2 - (3i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3-15i) \cdot (-2-3i)} {4 + 9} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3-15i) \cdot (-2-3i)} {13} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-3-15i}) \cdot (\red{-2-3i})} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-3} \cdot \red{(-2)} + \blue{-15} \cdot \red{(-2) i} + \blue{-3} \cdot \red{-3 i} + \blue{-15} \cdot \red{-3 i^2}} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{6 + 30i + 9i + 45 i^2} {13} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{6 + 30i + 9i - 45} {13} = \dfrac{-39 + 39i} {13} = -3+3i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{3+2i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-3+15i}{3-2i} = \dfrac{-3+15i}{3-2i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{3+2i}}{\green{3+2i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3+15i) \cdot (3+2i)} {3^2 - (-2i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3+15i) \cdot (3+2i)} {(3)^2 - (-2i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3+15i) \cdot (3+2i)} {9 + 4} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-3+15i) \cdot (3+2i)} {13} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-3+15i}) \cdot (\red{3+2i})} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-3} \cdot \red{3} + \blue{15} \cdot \red{3 i} + \blue{-3} \cdot \red{2 i} + \blue{15} \cdot \red{2 i^2}} {13} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-9 + 45i - 6i + 30 i^2} {13} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-9 + 45i - 6i - 30} {13} = \dfrac{-39 + 39i} {13} = -3+3i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-1+3i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-2-6i}{-1-3i} = \dfrac{-2-6i}{-1-3i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-1+3i}}{\green{-1+3i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-2-6i) \cdot (-1+3i)} {(-1)^2 - (-3i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-2-6i) \cdot (-1+3i)} {(-1)^2 - (-3i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-2-6i) \cdot (-1+3i)} {1 + 9} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-2-6i) \cdot (-1+3i)} {10} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-2-6i}) \cdot (\red{-1+3i})} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-2} \cdot \red{(-1)} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{(-1) i} + \blue{-2} \cdot \red{3 i} + \blue{-6} \cdot \red{3 i^2}} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{2 + 6i - 6i - 18 i^2} {10} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{2 + 6i - 6i + 18} {10} = \dfrac{20 + 0i} {10} = 2 $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-3+i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{2-16i}{-3-i} = \dfrac{2-16i}{-3-i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-3+i}}{\green{-3+i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-16i) \cdot (-3+i)} {(-3)^2 - (-i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-16i) \cdot (-3+i)} {(-3)^2 - (-i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-16i) \cdot (-3+i)} {9 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-16i) \cdot (-3+i)} {10} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{2-16i}) \cdot (\red{-3+i})} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{2} \cdot \red{(-3)} + \blue{-16} \cdot \red{(-3) i} + \blue{2} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{-16} \cdot \red{1 i^2}} {10} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-6 + 48i + 2i - 16 i^2} {10} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-6 + 48i + 2i + 16} {10} = \dfrac{10 + 50i} {10} = 1+5i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{1+i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{-1+5i}{1-i} = \dfrac{-1+5i}{1-i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{1+i}}{\green{1+i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-1+5i) \cdot (1+i)} {1^2 - (-i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-1+5i) \cdot (1+i)} {(1)^2 - (-i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-1+5i) \cdot (1+i)} {1 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(-1+5i) \cdot (1+i)} {2} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{-1+5i}) \cdot (\red{1+i})} {2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{-1} \cdot \red{1} + \blue{5} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{-1} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{5} \cdot \red{1 i^2}} {2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-1 + 5i - 1i + 5 i^2} {2} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-1 + 5i - 1i - 5} {2} = \dfrac{-6 + 4i} {2} = -3+2i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{-2+i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{1+8i}{-2-i} = \dfrac{1+8i}{-2-i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{-2+i}}{\green{-2+i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(1+8i) \cdot (-2+i)} {(-2)^2 - (-i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(1+8i) \cdot (-2+i)} {(-2)^2 - (-i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(1+8i) \cdot (-2+i)} {4 + 1} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(1+8i) \cdot (-2+i)} {5} $

 The denominator now doesn't contain any imaginary unit multiples, so it is a real number.

  Note that when a complex number, $a + bi$ is multiplied by its conjugate, the product is always $a^2 + b^2$.

Now, we can multiply out the two factors in the numerator. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{(\blue{1+8i}) \cdot (\red{-2+i})} {5} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{\blue{1} \cdot \red{(-2)} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{(-2) i} + \blue{1} \cdot \red{1 i} + \blue{8} \cdot \red{1 i^2}} {5} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{-2 - 16i + 1i + 8 i^2} {5} $

Finally, simplify the fraction. 

 $ \qquad \dfrac{-2 - 16i + 1i - 8} {5} = \dfrac{-10 - 15i} {5} = -2-3i $
We can divide complex numbers by multiplying both numerator and denominator by the denominator's complex conjugate, which is $\green{3-5i}$.
$\qquad \dfrac{2-8i}{3+5i} = \dfrac{2-8i}{3+5i} \cdot \dfrac{\green{3-5i}}{\green{3-5i}} $

We can simplify the denominator using the fact $(a + b) \cdot (a - b) = a^2 - b^2$. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-8i) \cdot (3-5i)} {3^2 - (5i)^2} $

Evaluate the squares in the denominator and subtract them. 

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-8i) \cdot (3-5i)} {(3)^2 - (5i)^2} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-8i) \cdot (3-5i)} {9 + 25} $

 $ \qquad = \dfrac{(2-8i) \cdot (3-5i)} {34} $
